--- Page 1 ---
1
Visibility-aware Satellite Selection and Resource Allocation in
Multi-Orbit LEO Networks
Yingzhuo Sun, Student Member, IEEE, Yulan Gao, Member, IEEE, Ming Xiao, Senior Member, IEEE, Zhu
Han, Fellow, IEEE, and Octavia A. Dobre, Fellow, IEEE
Abstract—Multi-orbit low-earth-orbit (M-LEO) satellites com-
munication is envisioned as a key infrastructure to deliver truly
global coverage, enabling future services from space–air–ground
integrated networks. However, the optimized design of M-LEO
which jointly addresses satellite selection, association control,
and resource scheduling while accounting for dynamic visibil-
ity in multi-orbit constellations still remains open. Satellites
moving along distinct orbital planes yield phase-shifted ground
tracks and heterogeneous, time-varying coverage patterns that
significantly complicate the optimization. To bridge the gap, we
propose a dynamic visibility-aware multi-orbit satellite selection
framework which can determine the optimal serving satellites
across orbital layers. The framework is built upon Markov
approximation and matching game theory to jointly address
the challenges of time-varying visible satellite sets, fluctuating
link quality due to varying visibility, and dynamic inter-user
interference. Specifically, we formulate a combinatorial optimiza-
tion problem that maximizes the sum-rate under per-satellite
power budgets and multi-orbit constellation size limitations.
The problem is NP-hard because it entails a mixed-integer
non-convex structure, combining discrete user association (UA)
decisions with continuous power allocation, and an inherently
non-convex sum-rate maximization objective. We address it
through a problem-specific Markov approximation. Moreover,
we alternately solve UA/bandwidth allocation via a matching-
game mechanism and power allocation via a Lagrangian dual
convex program, which together form a block coordinate descent
method tailored to this problem. Simulation results show that the
proposed algorithm converges to a suboptimal solution across
all scenarios. Extensive experiments against four state-of-the-art
baselines further demonstrate that our algorithm achieves, on
average, approximately 7.85% higher sum rate than the best-
performing baseline.
Index Terms—M-LEO, space–air–ground integrated networks,
Markov approximation, matching game theory.
I. INTRODUCTION
W
ITH ever-growing demand for higher data rates and
ubiquitous connectivity, the existing terrestrial net-
works are increasingly unable to satisfy the requirements
of future communication services [1], and leave users in
remote, rural, and maritime areas underserved due to the
lack of coverage [2]. To overcome these limitations, satellite
This paper has been presented in part at the IEEE International Conference
on Communications (ICC), 2025.
Y. Sun, Y. Gao, and M. Xiao are with the Division of Information Science
and Engineering, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, 100 44 Stockholm,
Sweden (e-mail: {yingzhuo, yulang, mingx}@kth.se).
Z.
Han
is
with
the
Department
of
Electrical
and
Computer
En-
gineering, University of Houston, Houston, TX 77004, USA (e-mail:
hanzhu22@gmail.com).
O. A. Dobre is with the Faculty of Engineering and Applied Sci-
ence, Memorial University, St. John’s, NL A1B 3X5, Canada (e-mail:
odobre@mun.ca).
communications have emerged as a promising complement to
terrestrial networks. In particular, multi-orbit low-earth-orbit
(M-LEO) satellite systems are envisioned as an infrastructure
to provide seamless connectivity with truly global coverage,
bridging underserved regions and enhancing the resilience of
space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) [3].
M-LEO systems are typically defined as satellites oper-
ating in orbits at altitudes between 500 km and 2,000 km.
Their primary advantages include low launch costs, and low
round-trip delay owing to the relatively short propagation
distance. However, deploying large-scale M-LEO constella-
tions inevitably introduces significant network complexity due
to the massive number of satellites and high velocity. In
particular, the resulting highly dynamic constellation leads
to a time-varying set of visible satellites over any given
region, thereby making adaptive satellite selection essential
for ensuring reliable connectivity to user equipment (UEs).
Extensive research efforts have been devoted to addressing
the challenges of satellite constellation design, user association
(UA), and resource allocation (RA). Among the early contri-
butions in this area, [4] developed a three-dimensional constel-
lation design algorithm to minimize the number of satellites
while ensuring backhaul requirements. Another corpus of
literature has focused on UA and RA under fixed constellation
settings. For instance, [5] formulated the UA problem between
ground UEs and LEO satellites, but the study assumed only
three fixed satellites and did not consider satellite selection.
Beyond fixed settings, several studies investigated satellite
selection strategies. To capture long-term selection dynamics,
[6] introduced a non-dominated sorting optimization algorithm
for M-LEO selection in global navigation satellite systems,
considering signal strength, and residual visibility time.
Despite these advances, most existing studies assume fixed
constellation, where the set of serving satellites is prede-
termined and static. These common assumptions may be
reasonable for simplified scenarios with a small number of
satellites, but they become unrealistic in large-scale M-LEO
systems. In practice, satellites in different orbital planes move
continuously. This leads to a dynamic and time-varying set
of visible satellites over any specific region. This dynamic
visibility introduces two critical challenges:
1) the number of visible satellites fluctuates significantly
over time, ranging from dense situations with many
candidate satellites to sparse conditions with only a few
feasible connections;
2) even if multiple satellites are simultaneously visible, their
link qualities vary due to differences in elevation angles,
residual visibility time, and interference conditions.
arXiv:2511.12678v1  [eess.SY]  16 Nov 2025


--- Page 2 ---
2
These factors imply the number of satellites to be selected and
the serving satellites must be optimized dynamically.
To address the challenges, we propose the dynamic
visibility-aware multi-orbit satellite selection framework (DV-
MOSS), which integrates constellation-level satellite adapta-
tion with fine-grained per-satellite selection across multiple
orbital layers. Particularly, we investigate a scenario in which a
UE is simultaneously covered by multiple candidate satellites.
Then the network controller must optimize the number of serv-
ing satellites, the satellite-UE associations, and the transmit
powers. The objective is to maximize the sum-rate under per-
satellite power budget constraints and overall constellation size
limitations. Notably, the number of visible M-LEO satellites
varies significantly across time slots. This dynamic visibility
makes the problem more challenging. The main innovations
and contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows.
• We investigate the sum-rate maximization problem of the
joint optimization of multi-orbit satellite selection, associ-
ation control, and PA under constellation size constraints
in an M-LEO setting. Different from existing works
assuming a fixed set of serving satellites, we explic-
itly model the temporal evolution of candidate satellite
sets. Dynamic visibility together with constellation sizes
changes the problem setting drastically. Since satellites in
different orbital planes and with varying relative phases
continuously enter and exit the cone-shaped visibility
regions of ground UEs, the feasible set of the problem is
inherently time-varying. The formulated problem is NP-
hard because it is a mixed-integer non-convex program:
discrete UA variables are coupled with continuous power
variables through a non-concave sum-rate objective and
a non-concave minimum-rate constraint.
• To solve the problem, we propose DV-MOSS algorithms
combining Markov approximation and matching game
theory. Specifically, we design an ergodic Markov chain
with tailored transition probabilities that guarantee con-
vergence to its stationary distribution. Building on this
reformulation, we develop a block coordinate descent
(BCD) architecture, termed JUBPA, for the joint opti-
mization of UE association, bandwidth allocation (BA),
and power allocation (PA). In particular, matching game
theory is employed to determine stable satellite-UE as-
sociations together with BA, whereas a Lagrangian dual-
based method is adopted to optimize PA.
This integrated approach achieves sub-optimal perfor-
mance with provable convergence of submodules and
significantly lower complexity than heuristic alternatives.
• We conduct extensive experiments on the Walker Constel-
lation Design Pattern1 to validate the convergence prop-
erties of DV-MOSS and to evaluate its performance. The
results demonstrate that DV-MOSS consistently outper-
forms the best-performing baseline, achieving an average
improvement of approximately 7.85% in sum rates.
1The Walker constellation has been widely adopted in practical de-
ployments, including Starlink, OneWeb, and Kuiper systems. It is avail-
able at https://innovationspace.ansys.com/courses/courses/intro-to-orbit-types/
lessons/walker-constellation-lesson-9/.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Sec-
tion II discusses the related work. Section III describes the
system model and problem formulation. The algorithm design
and simulation results are presented in Section IV and V,
respectively. Finally, the conclusions are drawn in Section VI.
II. RELATED WORK
Research on M-LEO satellite communications and RA in
SAGINs has gained significant attention in recent years. Prior
studies provide valuable insights into constellation design, UA,
and RA, as well as satellite selection strategies. However, most
of these works rely on simplified assumptions, such as fixed
satellite constellations or the separate optimization of UA and
RA, which limit applicability in large-scale, dynamic M-LEO
systems. Existing results can be roughly divided into four
categories, as follows.
1) Optimization with Fixed Constellations: A commonly
adopted paradigm for studying UA and RA in satellite net-
works is to assume a fixed LEO satellite constellation. For
instance, [5], [7]–[10] represent typical research based on this
paradigm. Reference [5] developed a 3D constellation coor-
dinate model and aimed to maximize the minimum average
throughput using an iterative algorithm that combines block
coordinate descent with successive convex approximation.
Similarly, [8] introduced a two-way transmission model with
unknown channel state information and designed a hierarchical
multi-agent multi-armed bandit-based RA scheme. In [9], UA
and RA were jointly optimized by formulating a problem
that incorporates both base station backhaul and UE traffic
demands, and is solved via binary relaxation and alternating
optimization. A deep reinforcement learning-based RA strat-
egy was further proposed in [10] to address low resource
utilization caused by uneven traffic demands.
2) Single-Satellite Selection Approaches: A few studies aim
to optimize selecting a single serving LEO satellite for each
UE. Specifically, [11] modeled the selection as a potential
game, treating satellites and frequency bands as shared re-
sources while UEs act as competing players. A random access
algorithm within a software-defined networking framework
was then developed to maximize the area sum-rate.
3) Multi-Satellite or Multi-Orbit Selection Methods: Beyond
single-satellite settings, the literature [12]–[14] investigated
the selection of multiple LEO satellites in static systems. In
[12], the authors proposed a two-layer algorithm that combines
difference-of-convex programming with coalition formation to
jointly address beamforming and UA. A comparative study in
[13] analyzed three UA schemes using stochastic geometry
as the evaluation framework. More advanced optimization
techniques were explored. Reference [14] applied alternat-
ing direction method of multipliers (ADMM), bisection, and
interior-point methods to jointly solve satellite selection and
access problems, targeting improvements in downlink sum-
rate, uplink fairness, and energy efficiency.
4) Long-Term Multi-Satellite Selection: A few works in-
vestigated long-term strategies for selecting multiple LEO
satellites. For instance, [6], [15]–[18] considered satellite
selection without explicit UA or RA. In [6], an iterative


--- Page 3 ---
3
optimization algorithm for the Global Navigation Satellite
System systems was developed, incorporating signal strength,
residual visibility time, and receiver sensitivity. The authors in
[15] addressed the coexistence between primary and secondary
systems via the Lagrange relaxation and sub-gradient meth-
ods. Reference [16] applied gated recurrent units to predict
trajectories and design handover strategies for high-speed
terminals. A dynamic greedy algorithm for handover based
on UE grouping was proposed in [17]. In [18], a conditional
handover optimization strategy using service continuity graphs
was designed to maximize a reward function and predict
handover sequences while improving the stability.
Meanwhile, references [19]–[23] incorporated UA or RA
alongside multi-satellite selection. In [19], a distributed mas-
sive multiple-input multiple-output-based network architecture
was introduced, and an artificial intelligence-enabled joint PA
and handover management scheme was proposed to enhance
throughput and reduce handovers. Reference [20] proposed
a collaborative beamforming paradigm with an evolutionary
multi-objective deep reinforcement learning algorithm, im-
proving uplink performance for energy-limited terminals. In
[21], the satellite-aircraft handover was modeled as a local
cooperation game, where subcarrier and PA were jointly
optimized via a parallel update strategy.
Caching was considered in [22], where a deep reinforce-
ment learning-based caching-aware handover strategy reduced
failures and blocking under limited resources. In [23], a
Lyapunov-based framework jointly optimized inter-satellite
handover, beam hopping, and spectrum sharing to improve
service satisfaction while mitigating interference.
Different from the above studies, the proposed DV-MOSS
does not assume fixed constellation or static satellite amount.
DV-MOSS is designed to handle dynamic visibility in M-
LEO systems by jointly optimizing satellite selection, UA, and
RA under constellation size constraints, combining Markov
approximation with matching game theory to tackle the chal-
lenges of time-varying candidate satellite sets and coupled
decision variables.
III. SYSTEM MODEL AND PROBLEM FORMULATION
A. Network Model
Consider the downlink transmission of an M-LEO satellite
network where satellites distributed across O orbital planes
adaptively serve J UEs within a designated area. Let O =
{1, . . . , O} and U = {u1, . . . , uJ} denote the sets of orbit
indices and UEs, respectively. Let S = ∪O
o=1So denote the set
of satellites across O orbital planes. So = {so,1, . . . , so,|So|}
represents the set of satellites on the o-th orbital plane, and
|So| denotes its cardinality. The downlink spectrum is assumed
to be partitioned into K orthogonal and equal-bandwidth
subcarriers that are represented by the set B = {B1, . . . , BK}.
B. Zenith Angle Rule
The zenith angle of the satellites relative to UE is an
important metric for satellite selection, as it directly influences
the communication quality [24]. To maintain line-of-sight
(LoS) communication links, blockages caused by tall buildings
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗1
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗2
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗3
𝑆𝑆𝑗𝑗1(𝑡𝑡)
𝑆𝑆𝑗𝑗3(𝑡𝑡)
𝑆𝑆𝑗𝑗2(𝑡𝑡)
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗4
𝑆𝑆𝑗𝑗4(𝑡𝑡)
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗5
𝑆𝑆𝑗𝑗5(𝑡𝑡)
𝜑𝜑
x
y
z
𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗4
Not covered satellites
Potential satellites
covered by 𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗4’s cone
 
The 𝑗𝑗4-th UE
The cone of 𝑢𝑢𝑗𝑗4
Fig. 1. Illustration of the zenith angle rule.
should be avoided. A common method is to set a zenith-angle
threshold when selecting satellites. Let φ denote the maximum
zenith angle at which a UE can observe satellites. This
threshold naturally defines a cone-shaped visibility region, and
the satellites within this cone for uj constitute the candidate set
Sj(t). The cone-shaped visibility region and the corresponding
candidate set Sj(t) are illustrated in Fig. 1. Based on the setup
origin point, the coordinates of uj, so,i, and the earth center are
denoted as aj(t), ao,i(t), and ae, respectively. Accordingly,
Sj(t) can be defined as
Sj(t) = {so,i | ⟨aj(t) −ae, ao,i(t) −aj(t)⟩≤cos φ},
(1)
where ⟨·, ·⟩represents the operator of calculating the nor-
malized inner product between two vectors. All satellites
located within the individual cone regions collectively form
the union set are denoted as ˜S(t) = ∪jSj(t). The serving
satellites are then selected from ˜S(t). To indicate whether
a specific satellite so,i is actively providing service at time
t, we introduce a binary decision variable zo,i(t) ∈{0, 1}.
zo,i(t) = 1 iff so,i is selected; otherwise, zo,i(t) = 0. The
final set of optimized satellites is denoted as ˜S∗(t) ⊆˜S(t).
Therefore, the set of satellites providing service at time slot t
is ˜S∗(t) = {so,i | zo,i(t) = 1}.
C. Transmission Model
It is important to note that only the satellites selected to
provide service are eligible for BA. The set of subcarriers
assigned to so,i is denoted by Bo,i. The sets fulfill the
conditions B = ∪so,i∈˜
SBo,i(t), and |Bo,i(t)| = K/| ˜S∗(t)|.
We further assume that the frequency bands assigned to
different service-providing satellites are mutually exclusive
[25]. In other words, there is no overlap between Bo,i(t) and
Bo,i′(t) for i ̸= i′, i.e., Bo,i(t) ∩Bo′,i′(t) = ∅, ∀(o, i) ̸=
(o′, i′)|zo,i=zo′,i′=1.
To characterize the connection relationships, we introduce
two additional binary variables. The UA indicator is denoted as
xo,i,j(t) ∈{0, 1}, where xo,i,j(t) = 1 if so,i serves uj at time
slot t, and xo,i,j(t) = 0 otherwise. Likewise, the BA indicator
is denoted as yk
o,i,j(t) ∈{0, 1} with yk
o,i,j(t) = 1 if frequency
band Bk is allocated to serve uj, and yk
o,i,j(t) = 0 otherwise.
Hence, the set of UEs associated with so,i is Uo,i(t) = {uj |
xo,i,j(t) = 1}, while the set of UEs served by Bk is denoted
as Uk(t) = {uj | yk
o,i,j(t) = 1}.

The channel coefficient between uj and so,i at time t is
Ho,i,j(t) =
q
PLo,i,j(t) · SF(t) · Gtho,i,j(t),
(2)
where Gt denotes the transmitting antenna gain, SF(t) and
ho,i,j(t) represent the shadowing fading and the small scale
fading channel coefficient between so,i and uj, respectively.
The path loss PLo,i,j(t) between so,i and uj is given by
PLo,i,j(t) = C1 · log10 do,i,j(t) + C2 + C3 · log10 fc,
(3)
where do,i,j(t) represents the Euclidean distance between
so,i and uj, and fc is the carrier frequency. The parame-
ters C1, C2, and C3 can be referred from tables provided
in QuaDRiGa [26]. The small scale fading is calculated
as ho,i,j(t) = ej2πfcdo,i,j(t). The signal-to-interference-plus-
noise ratio (SINR) experienced by uj on the k-th subcarrier
from its serving satellite so,i at time t is
Γk

(6)

D. Problem Formulation
As mentioned in Section III.B, our setting explicitly ac-
counts for dynamic visibility induced by each UE’s cone-
shaped field of view. As satellites on different orbital planes
and relative phases enter/exit these cones, each per-UE candi-
date set Sj(t), ∀uj ∈U, along with their union ˜S(t), evolves
across time slots. Hence, service satellites must be adapted
online under constellation size limits and per-satellite power
budgets. This time-varying feasible region tightly couples
satellite selection, UA and PA, and substantially enlarges
the combinatorial search space. Consequently, our problem
includes both dynamic visibility and constellation size con-
straints, leading to the formulation of an optimization problem
that simultaneously addresses satellite selection, UA, and RA,
as below
OP-0 : max
x,y,z,p
X
t∈[0,T ] R(t)
(9)
s.t.
X
o,i xo,i,j(t) · zo,i(t) ≤1,
∀j,
(10)
XJ
j=1 xo,i,j(t) · zo,i(t) · pj(t) ≤Pmax,
∀o, i, (11)
X
o,i zo,i(t) ≤Zth,
(12)
rj(t) ≥rmin,j(t),
∀j,
(13)
pj(t) > 0,
∀j,
(14)
so,i(t) ∈˜S(t),
∀(o, i)|zo,i=1,
(15)
xo,i,j(t), yk
o,i,j(t), zo,i(t) ∈{0, 1} ,
∀o, i, j, k,
(16)
where (10) enforces that each UE can be associated with at
most one satellite. (11) ensures that the transmit power of each
satellite does not exceed its maximum limit. (12) represents
the constellation size constraint. Therefore the number of the
chosen satellites should not be larger than Zth. (13) requires
that the minimum rate demand of every UE is satisfied. (14)
specifies that the allocated power to each UE must remain
positive. (15) guarantees that the service satellites are selected
from the union set defined by the cone-based visibility rule.
Problem OP-0 has integer constraints (10)-(12) and (15)-
(16), as well as continuous constraints (11) and (13)-(14).
The problem is non-convex and mixed integer programming
with respect to {x, y, z, p}, where x = [xo,i,j]∀o,i,j, y =
[yk
o,i,j]∀o,i,j,k and z = [zo,i]∀o,i, p = [pj]∀j. In the absence of
efficient solvers for the problem, we adopt a tractable alter-
native. Specifically, we will use a problem-specific Markov
approximation that exploits the combinatorial structure for
sub-optimal solutions with manageable complexity as follows.
IV. THE PROPOSED SOLUTION FRAMEWORK
To effectively solve problem OP-0, we propose DV-MOSS
framework which integrates four complementary components
into a unified solution pipeline. First, Markov approximation
is applied to address the combinatorial nature of satellite
selection under constellation size constraints and dynamic
visibility variations. Then we reformulate the problem into
a tractable form with guaranteed convergence to a stationary
distribution. Next, based on the selected satellite set, a BCD
structure is introduced to solve UA, BA, and PA alternatively.
Specifically, two matching games are utilized to establish
stable UA with BA. Conditioned on the outcome of UA and
BA, PA is optimized using KKT conditions. By combining
these components in a sequential yet coherent manner, DV-
MOSS becomes a scalable and suboptimal framework for the
joint satellite selection and joint UA, BA, and PA. The detail
of each component will be elaborated in the following.
A. Markov Approximation
Let f
= {x, y, z, p} ∈F denote a feasible M-LEO
satellites network configuration with F as the feasible set
generated by the constraints (10)-(16). The objective function
in (9) can be reevaluated as θ(f) = P
t∈[0,T ] R(t) with
f ∈F. Accordingly, the original objective function can be
equivalently reformulated as
OP-1 : maxf∈F
θ(f).
(17)
OP-1 is inherently discrete and combinatorial. To make it
tractable, we approximate the problem by reformulating it
as a continuous optimization problem through log-sum-exp
relaxation and the use of a conjugate function [27] as
OP-2 :
max
w
X
f i∈F
wf iθ(f i) −1
β
X
f i∈F
wf i log(wf i)
(18)


--- Page 5 ---
5
s.t.
X
f i∈F wf i = 1,
(19)
where wf i denotes the weight of a feasible network config-
uration f i, and β is a temperature parameter controlling the
difference between the objective function value of OP-1 and
that of OP-2. KKT condition for OP-2 leads to the optimal
weights as
w∗
f i =
eβθ(f i)
P|F|
m=1 eβθ(f m)) .
(20)
However, it is not practical to compute the optimal weights
for all feasible network configurations. To address this chal-
lenge, we regard the feasible configurations as states and
the corresponding weight as the stationary probability of a
Markov chain. The problem then becomes the design of a
Markov chain with F as the state space and the stationary
distribution as (20). We note that there is at least one Markov
chain fulfilling our requirements [27]. Let q(f →f ′) and
q(f ′ →f) denote the transition probabilities. The Markov
chain should satisfy the balance condition and is expressed as
wfq(f →f ′) = wf ′q(f ′ →f),
(21)
which is derived according to the Bayes rule on a joint
probability of f and f ′. After substituting (20) into (21) and
canceling out the denominators on both sides, we can obtain
eβθ(f)q(f →f ′) = eβθ(f ′)q(f ′ →f).
(22)
We assume q(f →f ′) + q(f ′ →f) = 1 in Markov approxi-
mation [27]. The transition probabilities can be evaluated as
q(f →f ′) =
1
1 + eβ(θ(f)−θ(f ′)) ,
(23)
q(f ′ →f) =
1
1 + eβ(θ(f ′)−θ(f)) .
(24)
With the state space specified and the transition probabilities
given in (23)–(24), we summarize the Markov-approximation
procedure in Algorithm 1. The algorithm iteratively alternates
between an exploration stage and a consolidation stage. In each
iteration, Algorithm 2 is invoked to determine UA, BA, and PA
based on the currently selected satellites. If the state stabilizes
(i.e., remains unchanged across iterations), the exploration
probability ν[co] will be gradually reduced. Algorithm 1 ter-
minates once the exploration probability decreases to zero, at
which point a stable solution is obtained.
Before executing Algorithm 2, it is necessary to find a
feasibly initialized solution point. For the purpose, we will
find a solution point at the boundary line generated by the
minimum rate constraint (13). Thus, we let the communication
rate of each uj equal to rmin,j.
After substituting (8) into the equation rj(t) = rmin,j(t)
and omitting the indexes o, i, t for simplicity, rmin,j can be
evaluated as
rmin,j = B·log2

1 +
pj|Hk
j |2
P
j′,yk
j′=1 pj′ · |Hk
j |2 + σ2

, ∀j, (25)
which is equivalent to
pj · 1
δj
−
X
j′,yk
j′=1 pj′ = ϵj,k,
∀j,
(26)
where δj = 2
rj,min
B
−1, ϵj,k =
σ2
|Hk
j |2 . From (26), for each
Algorithm 1 The DV-MOSS Framework
1: Initialization: co = 0, ν[0] = 1, βstep > 0, νstep > 0, z[0]
drawn at random.
2: while ν[co] ̸= 0 do
3:
if
mod (co, 2) = 0 then
4:
% Exploration stage
5:
z[co+1] =
(
z
drawn randomly,
with prob. ν[co]
z[co],
with prob. 1 −ν[co]
6:
else
7:
% Consolidation stage
8:
Calculate α = q(f [co] →f [co−1]) based on (23).
9:
f [co+1] =
(
f [co−1],
with prob. α
f [co],
with prob. 1 −α
10:
β = β + βstep
11:
if z[co+1] = z[co] then
12:
ν[co+1] = max
n
0, ν[co] −νstep
o
13:
f [co+1]∗
=
n
x[co+1]∗, y[co+1]∗, p[co+1]∗, z[co+1]o
by
running JUBPA (Algorithm 2)
14:
co = co + 1
15: Return z∗,x∗,y∗,p∗
Algorithm 2 Algorithm of JUBPA
Output: x[co+1]∗, y[co+1]∗, p[co+1]∗
Input: z[co+1]
1: for uj in Linit
UE do
2:
for Bk in Linit

do
3:

4:

uj
	
, Break
5:

7: while (x, y) is not stable do
8:
UA+BA Phase: Run Algorithm 3 to obtain x[co+1,ci]
and y[co+1,ci]

PA Phase: Run Algorithm 4 to obtain p[co+1,ci]
10:
ci = ci + 1
11: Return x[co+1]∗, y[co+1]∗, p[co+1]∗
subcarrier Bk we obtain a system of the first-degree linear
equations as
Gk = G(Uk) : Akpk = ϵk, ∀k,
(27)
where Uk denotes the UE set served by Bk, G(Uk) denotes
the linear equations system induced by the UE set Uk. The
UE power vector pk ∈RJk×1 is defined as [pk]jk = pjk, and
ϵk ∈RJk×1 is defined as [ϵk]jk = ϵk
jk. Jk = P
jk yk
jk is the
number of UEs served by Bk. Ak ∈RJk×Jk is defined as
[Ak]ij =
(
1
δj ,
i = j,
−1,
i ̸= j.
(28)
To model the incremental augmentation of Uk when uj is
assigned to Bk, we define the augmentation operator as
G+(Gk, uj) : Aj→kpj→k = ϵj→k,
(29)
which maps the previous linear system of Bk and the candidate
UE uj to the updated linear system after augmentation.
pj→k ∈R(Jk+1)×1 is defined as pj→k = [pT
k , pj]T , and
ϵj→k ∈R(Jk+1)×1 is defined as ϵj→k = [ϵT
k , ϵj,k]T . The


--- Page 6 ---
6
updated matrix Aj→k ∈R(Jk+1)×(Jk+1) is defined as

Aj→k

i′j′ =







[Ak]i′j′ ,
i′, j′ ≤Jk,
1/δj,
i′ = j′ = Jk + 1,

others
(30)
The definition of solvable G+(Gk, uj) is
G+(Gk, uj) is solvable ⇐⇒det
␀Aj→k

̸= 0,
␀Aj→k
−1 ϵj→k > 0.
(31)
In the initialization of JUBPA, the UE with higher rmin,j/¯gj(t)
is prioritized to be allocated to the subcarrier with the highest
channel gain. This is because the UE with higher rmin,j/¯gj(t)
has more stringent minimum rate requirements but experiences
low channel gains. As a result, satisfying the constraint on the
minimum rate for such UEs is challenging.
B. Matching Game Theory
With a feasible solution from the initialization process, we
will optimize UA and BA with the matching game method,
OP-UABA : max
x,y
R(t)
(32)
s.t. (10), (11), (13), (16).
(33)
In the following, we design two matching games correspond-
ing to UA and BA, respectively. Both matching games are
designed based on the explored z[co+1] and the corresponding
satellites set is defined as Sz[co+1](t) which will be denoted
as Sz(t) for simplicity in the following. There are two sides
of players in each matching game. In the UA matching game,
two sides are UEs and satellites, and the corresponding sets
are U and Sz(t). In the BA matching game, two sides are UEs
associated with so,i and subcarriers belonging to so,i, and the
corresponding sets are Uo,i and Bo,i. The matching function is
used to describe the mapping relationship between two sides
of players in each game. The matching function µUA(·) for
UA is defined as a function mapping U to Sz(t) such that
so,i = µUA(uj) iff uj ∈µ−1
UA(so,i), in which µ−1
UA(so,i) is
defined as
µ−1
UA(so,i; t) = Uo,i =

uj | xo,i,j(t) = 1
	
.
(34)
Likewise, the matching µBA(·) for BA is defined as a function
mapping Uo,i to Bo,i such that Bk = µBA(uj) iff uj ∈
µ−1
BA(Bk), in which µ−1
BA(Bk) is defined as
µ−1
BA(Bk; t) = Uk = {uj | yk
o,i,j(t) = 1}.
(35)
The final matching result of UA and BA is denoted as µ =
{µUA, µBA}. After defining basic elements in a matching game,
how to design a matching game to obtain a stable matching
result is introduced as the following.
The general procedure of a matching game can be summa-
rized as three steps:
1) Both sides of players construct the profile lists based on
the preference function;
2) Each player on the first side proposes the requirement
of being associated with the player corresponding to the
highest preference function on the second side;
3) Each player on the second side decides whether to be
associated with the first-side player that proposed the
association requirement based on the profile list.
In order to apply matching game to solve the problem OP-
UABA, we need to design the preference functions that are
related to the objective function and constraints in OP-UABA.
In the UA matching game, the preference function on the
UE side is designed based on the objective function, and the
preference function on satellites side is designed based on the
constraints. In the BA matching game, the preference function
is designed based on objective function. The constraints are
handled in a single step where we check whether they are
satisfied. The pseudo code is shown in Algorithm 3.
1) UA: As mentioned above, the preference function on
the UE side is designed based on the objective function value.
Thus we define the preference function as the potential average
increment of each UE’s communication rate after changing the
associated satellite.
The rate of uj when it is served by Bk is defined as
rj→k(t) = yk
j (t)rj(t)+
(1 −yk
j (t))B log2




1 +
pj(t) |Hk
j (t)|2
X
j′∈Uk
pj′(t) |Hk
j (t)|2 + σ2




. (36)
The set of subcarriers belonging to so,i which can make
rj→k(t) higher than rj(t) is denoted as
B+
j→o,i =

Bk | rj→k(t) ≥rj(t), Bk ∈Bo,i
	
.
(37)
The profile function of uj is defined as
PF UA
j
(o, i; µ, t) =
1
|B+
j→o,i|
X
k∈B+
j→o,i
␀rj→k(t) −rj(t)

.
(38)
Therefore, if the subcarriers of so,i can make the communica-
tion rate of uj higher after serving uj, so,i will correspond to
a higher preference function value. The comparison operator
for uj at UA stage is defined as
(so,i, µUA) ≻UA
j;t (so′,i′, µ′
UA) ⇔
PF UA
j
(o, i; µ, t) ≥PF UA
j
(o′, i′; µ′, t).
(39)
The profile list of uj is denoted as LUA
j (t), representing a list
of satellites. LUA
j (t) is sorted in descending order according
to (39), i.e.,
LUA
j (t) = argsort
so,i∈Sz
PF UA
j
(o, i),
(40)
The maximum average achievable rate of uj can be derived
as γj(t) = maxso,i∈LUA
j (t) PF UA
j
(o, i).
To avoid oscillatory UA updates, we introduce two stabiliza-
tion mechanisms. The first one is per-iteration quota on UA
changes. Because one UE’s association change perturbs the
interference of others, allowing UEs to change associations
concurrently can induce large network-wide fluctuations and
hinder convergence. Hence, in each iteration only a quota-
limited subset of UEs is permitted to propose a change. Define


--- Page 7 ---
7
Algorithm 3 Algorithm of UA and BA
Output: x[co+1,ci],y[co+1,ci]
Input: x[co+1,ci−1], y[co+1,ci−1], p[co+1,ci−1]
1: % UA part
2: for each uj do
3:
Update LUA,[cin]
j
based on PF UA
j
(o, i; µ[cin−1])
4: Generate Lγ(t) and UUA
req
5: while UUA
req ̸= ∅do
6:
for uj ∈UUA
req do
7:
if Uj ≤Umax and µUA(uj) ̸= so∗,i∗then
8:

j
(o, i),bj→o∗,i∗= 1
9:
for each so,i ∈Sz do
10:
U req

o,i = P
uj∈Ureq
o,i pj
11:
if po,i + preq
o,i ≤pmax then
12:
Uo,i ←Ureq

o,i
13:
Ureq

req ←UUA
req \ {uj | uj ∈UUA
req }
14:
else
15:
while po,i < pmax do
16:
j′ = arg maxuj∈LUA
o,i
P

o,i (j)
17:
Uo,i ←Uo,i ∪{uj′}, po,i ←po,i + pj′
18:
UUA
req \ {uj′},Ureq
o,i \ {uj′},LUA
o,i \ {uj′}
19:
for uj ∈Urej
o,i do
20:
LUA,[cin]
j
←LUA,[cin]
j
\ {so,i}
21: % BA part
22: for so,i ∈SUA,[ci] do
23:
The list LUA,[ci]
o,i
= argsortuj∈U
UA,[ci]
o,i
pj(t)
24:
for uj ∈LUA,[ci]

do
25:
The sorted list Lo,i

= argsortBk∈Bo,i gk
j (t)
26:
for Bk ∈Lo,i
j
do
27:
if constraint (13) in Bk∗is fulfilled then
28:
yk
o,i,j(t) = 1
29:
else
30:
xo,i,j(t) = 0, x[ci−1]
o,i,j
= 1, Uj−= 1
31: for each so,i ∈Sz do
32:
while max PF BA(j, k) > 0 do
33:
UBA
req ←∅,Bblock ←∅
34:
for each Bk ∈Bo,i do
35:
UBA
req ←UBA
req ∪

arg minuj∈Uk rj(t)
	
36:
while max LBA ≥0 do
37:
(j∗, k∗) = arg maxj,k∈LBA PF BA(j, k)
38:
if

µBA(j∗), Bk∗	
/∈Bblock then
39:
if constraint (13) in Bk∗is fulfilled then
40:
Uk∗←Uk∗∪uj∗,UµBA(j∗) ←UµBA(j∗) \ j∗
41:
Bblock ←Bblock ∪

µBA(j∗), Bk∗	
42:
LBA ←LBA \

(j∗, k∗)
	
this subset of UEs as
Lγ(t) = argsort
uj∈U
γj(t).
(41)
where γj(t) = maxso,i∈LUA
j (t) PF UA
j
(o, i). Given a quota Uq,
only the first Uq UEs in Lγ(t) are allowed to request a UA
update. This set is denoted as UUA
req and is defined as
UUA
req = {uj ∈Lγ(t) | 1 ≤j ≤Uq}.
(42)
The second mechanism is per-UE limit on the number of UA
changes. To prevent back-and-forth switching by any single
UE, we impose a hard limit on its total number of association
updates. Let Uj be the cumulative count of uj changing UA.
A proposal from uj is accepted only if Uj < Umax. Umax is an
integer limit.
After UE proposing the request of changing association,
satellites will decide whether to accept the requests according
to their preference function. To define the preference function
on satellites’ side, from (13), the minimum-rate constraint of
uj on subcarrier Bk can be equivalently written as
pj(t)/δj −ϵj,k ≥
X
j′̸=j,µ(uj′;t)=Bk pj′(t).
(43)
We denote the left-hand term as
ak
j (t) = pj(t)/δj −ϵj,k
(44)
as the advantage score of uj on Bk. This quantity depends only
on the intrinsic parameters of uj: the transmit power pj(t)
and the minimum-rate requirement via δj, and the channel
gain term ϵk
j . A larger ak
j (t) implies a greater SINR surplus
for uj and therefore makes (43) easier to satisfy for any
fixed cochannel environment. In particular, ak
j (t) increases
with higher power and better channel gain, and decreases
with a stricter minimum-rate requirement, exactly matching
the intuition of an advantage intrinsic to uj.
The right-hand side (RHS) of (43), P
j′̸=j pj′(t) aggregates
the transmit powers of the other UEs sharing Bk. A larger RHS
tightens the inequality and thus makes UE uj’s minimum-
rate constraint harder to satisfy. By symmetry, the transmit
power of uj itself appears in the RHS of the constraints of
all other UEs on Bk, i.e., pj(t) increases their cochannel load
and degrades their feasibility. Hence pj(t) plays the role of an
external burden on its neighbors and is naturally interpreted
as a penalty term from the system perspective. Accordingly,
we define the preference function as a weighted difference
between these two components, i.e.,
PF UA
o,i (j; t) = φUA
X
k∈Bo,i
ak
j (t) −
X
k∈Bo,i
ck · pj(t),
(45)
where φUA is a scaling coefficient that modulates the nu-
merical contribution of the advantage score, and ck is the
cost per unit of the interference caused by uj [28]. With
this definition, a larger advantage score aj,k(t) increases the
preference, whereas a higher cochannel load reduces it.
The corresponding comparison function for satellites is
obtained as
(j, µUA) ≻UA
o,i;t (j′, µ′
UA) ⇔PF UA
o,i (j; t) ≥PF UA
o,i (j′; t). (46)
The profile list of so,i sorting UEs is denoted as LUA
o,i(t), and
can be defined as
LUA
o,i(t) = argsort
uj∈Uo,i
PF UA
o,i (j).
(47)
After executing the UA module (Algorithm 3, lines 1–19), we
can have the set of UEs which change associated satellites as
UUA,[ci]
o,i
=

uj | x[co+1,ci]
o,i,j
(t) = 1, x[co+1,ci−1]
o,i,j
(t) = 0
	
.
(48)
Through the definition of UUA,[ci]
o,i
, we can evaluate the set of


--- Page 8 ---
8
satellites which serve more UEs than the previous round as
SUA,[ci] = {so,i | UUA,[ci]
o,i
̸= ∅}.
(49)
2) BA: Because changing a UE’s associated subcarrier
will impact two subcarriers’ sum rates, i.e., the previously
associated subcarrier and the newly associated subcarrier. It
is necessary to design the preference function considering
impacts on these two subcarriers. The preference function
PF1BA(j; µ) is introduced to describe the impact on the
previously associated subcarrier and is calculated as
PF1BA(j; µ) = RµBA(j)

µ−1
BA(µBA(j)) \ j

−RµBA(j), (50)
where µ−1
BA(k) \ j denotes the set of UEs in Bk excluding
uj. RµBA(j)

µ−1
BA(µBA(j)) \ j

denotes the sum rate of UEs
served by the subcarrier µBA(j) excluding uj. It can be
calculated through introducing the function Rk (Ux). The sum
rate of UEs in Ux achieved on the Bk is defined as
Rk (Ux) =
X
j∈Ux B
× log2

1 +
pj(t)|Hk
j (t)|2
X
j′̸=j,j′∈Ux pj′(t)|Hk
j (t)|2 + σ2

.
(51)
The preference function PF2BA(j, k; µ) is introduced to de-
scribe the impact on the newly associated subcarrier and is
calculated as
PF2BA(j, k; µ) = Rk

µ−1
BA(k) ∪j

−Rk,
(52)
where µ−1
BA(k)∪uj denotes the set of UEs in Bk plus uj. The
profile function that is used to decide the profile list can be
defined as the sum of PF1BA(j; µ) and PF2BA(j, k; µ)
PF BA(j, k; µ) = PF1BA(j; µ) + PF2BA(j, k; µ).
(53)
The sorted profile list can then be constructed based on
PF(j, k; µ), and is denoted as LBA
o,i(t). The BA profile list
is constructed as
LBA
o,i(t) =
argsort
uj∈Uo,i,Bk∈Bo,i
PF BA(j, k).
(54)
Having detailed the UA and BA matching procedures, we
analyze their fundamental properties. In particular, we prove
that the matching results are stable and that the objective in-
creases monotonically across iterations, ensuring convergence
to a sub-optimal solution. It is formalized in Theorem 1.
Theorem 1. The matching µ = {µUA, µBA} is stable and can
attain a sub-optimal solution given the transmitted power.
Proof. According to the operation at the 14th line in the
UA stage of Algorithm 3, the matching µUA can make
the quantity P
j PF UA
o,i (j) increase after each iteration, i.e.,
P
j PF UA
o,i (j)[r+1] ≥P
j PF UA
o,i (j)[r]. It has been proved
that R(t) is non-decreasing with P
j PF UA
o,i (j) [28], then we
have R(x[r+1]; y, z, p, t) ≥R(x[r]; y, z, p, t). Clearly, µBA
can also guarantee the increment of the network through-
put given the UA result and transmitted power. Then we
have R(y[r+1]; x, z, p, t)
≥
R(y[r]; x, z, p, t). With the
UA matching results, we have R(x[co+1,ci],y[co+1,ci]; p, t) ≥
R(x[co+1,ci−1],y[co+1,ci−1]; p, t).
C. Lagrangian Dual-Based Optimization for PA
After UA and BA stages, the remaining variables to be
optimized are the UE transmit power p. Moreover, for both
the objective function and the constraints, the optimization
problem OP-0 can be reformulated as the form of the additive
sum over satellites as
OP-PA0 : max
p
X
(o,i)
X
j∈Uo,i log2 (1 + Γk
j )
(55)
s.t.
X
j∈Uo,i pj(t) ≤Pmax,
∀o, i,
(56)
rj(t) ≥rmin,j(t),
∀j ∈Uo,i, ∀o, i,
(57)
pj(t) > 0,
∀j ∈Uo,i, ∀o, i.
(58)
Because both the objective function (55) and the feasible set
decided by constraints (56)-(58) decompose across satellites,
the problem OP-PA0 can be separated into |Sz| sub-problems
corresponding to |Sz| satellites. The sub-problem correspond-
ing to so,i is denoted as OP-PA0so,i and formulated as
OP-PA0so,i : max
p
X
j∈Uo,i log2 (1 + Γk
j )
(59)
s.t.
X
j∈Uo,i pj ≤Pmax,
(60)
rj ≥rmin,j,
∀j ∈Uo,i,
(61)
pj ≥0,
∀j ∈Uo,i.
(62)
Then we can further transform this problem through geometric
programming. Let log2 (1 + Γk
j (t)) ≃log2 (Γk
j (t)), ´pj =
log2 pj, and ´Ik
j = log2 Ik
j , then OP-PA0so,i is transformed
to OP-PA1so,i as
OP-PA1so,i : min
´p,´I
X
j∈Uo,i log2
 2−´pj
|Hk
j |2 (2
´Ik
j + σ2)

(63)
s.t.
X
j∈Uo,i 2´pj ≤Pmax,
(64)
log2
2−´pj(2
´Ik
j + σ2)
|Hk
j (t)|2

+ χj ≤0,
∀j ∈Uo,i,
(65)
−´Ik
j + log2 Ik
j = 0,
∀j ∈Uo,i,
(66)
where χj = rmin,j
B , ∀j ∈Uo,i. By invoking the KKT framework
and introducing the multipliers λo,i, n, and m for the con-
straints (56), (57), and (58), respectively, OP-PA1so,i can be
transformed to Lagrangian saddle-point problem OP-PA2so,i,
i.e.,
OP-PA2so,i : min
´p,´I
max
λo,i,n,m
Lo,i
(67)
s.t. λo,i ≥0, n ≥0, m ≥0,
(68)
where Lagrange term Lo,i can be calculated as
Lo,i =
X
j∈Uo,i
log2
 2−´pj
|Hk
j |2 (2
´Ik
j + σ2)

+ λo,i(
X
j∈Uo,i 2´pj −Pmax)
+
X
j∈Uo,i
nj

log2
 2−´pj
|Hk
j |2 (2
´Ik
j + σ2)

+ χj

+
X
j∈Uo,i mj(−´Ik
j + log2 Ik
j ).
(69)
To solve OP-PA2so,i, we apply the duality function evalu-


--- Page 9 ---
9
Algorithm 4 Algorithm of PA
Output: p[co+1,ci]
Input: x[co+1,ci], y[co+1,ci], p[co+1,ci−1]
1: Initialization: λ(0), n(0), m(0), p(0), I(0)
2: for each so,i ∈Sz do
3:
r = 0
4:
while ´p does not converge do
5:
if r == 0 then
6:
Update λ(1)
o,i , n(1)
j
and m(1)
j
according to (76), (77)
and (78) based on the values of p(0), I(0)
7:
else
8:
Update ´p(r)∗
j
and ´Ik,(r)∗
j
based on (74) and (75)
9:
Calculate the value of Ik,(r)
j
based on the value of
´p(r)∗according to (5)
10:
Update λ(r+1)
o,i
, n(r+1)
j
and m(r+1)
j
according to
(76), (77) and (78) based on the values of ´p(r)∗,
Ik,(r)
j
and ´Ik,(r)∗
j
11:
r = r + 1
ated as Do,i(λo,i, n, m) = min´p,´I Lo,i.
Then the duality problem can be defined as
OP-PA3so,i :
max
λo,i,n,m
Do,i(λo,i, n, m)
(70)
s.t. λo,i ≥0, n ≥0, m ≥0.
(71)
Since the strong duality holds here [28], OP-PA3so,i is equiva-
lent to OP-PA2so,i and the optimal primal and dual variables ´p
and ´I are characterized by the saddle point of the Lagrangian.
Fixing the multipliers λo,i, n, and m, we apply the KKT
stationary conditions by differentiating Lo,i w.r.t. ´p and ´I and
setting the results to zero as
∂Lo,i/∂´pj = −(1 + nj) + λo,i2´pj · ln 2 = 0,
(72)
∂Lo,i/∂´Ik
j = (1 + nj)2
´Ik
j /(2
´Ik
j + σ2) −mj = 0.
(73)
Then the optimal pj and Ik
j are evaluated as
p∗
j = 2´p∗
j = (1 + nj)/(λo,i ln 2),
(74)
Ik∗
j
= 2
´Ik∗
j
= max{mjσ2/(1 + nj −mj), 0}.
(75)
Based on the optimal p∗
j and Ik∗
j , Lagrange multipliers λo,i,
nj and mj can be updated recursively with step sizes q(r)
λ , q(r)
n
and q(r)
m according to the subgradient method as
λ(r)
o,i = λ(r−1)
o,i
+ q(r)
λ
· g(r−1)
λo,i
,
(76)

obtaining the transmit power ´p(r−1)∗, i.e.,
Ik,(r−1)

as Ik,(r−1)
j
(´p(r−1)∗)
to emphasize its functional dependence on the power vector.
Since every part of JUBPA has been illustrated, we will
analyze the convergence of JUBPA by proposing the following
Theorem 2.
Theorem 2. Algorithm 2 can converge and achieve a sub-
optimal solution.
Proof. To show the convergence of Algorithm 2, we need to
prove the monotonicity of network rate function R(·) along
iteration steps, which is the function of x, y, and z. The first
inequality corresponding to PA stage is
R(p[co+1,ci])|[x[co+1,ci],y[co+1,ci]] ≥
R(p[co+1,ci−1])|[x[co+1,ci],y[co+1,ci]],
(83)
where R(⋆)|[▲] represents the network rate function w.r.t ⋆
under fixed parameters ▲. The inequality holds because the
PA part is designed based on the gradient type of method.
Thus the non-decreasing monotonicity can be achieved with
respect to p. The second inequality corresponding to UA and
BA stages is expressed as
R(x[co+1,ci], y[co+1,ci])|{p[co+1,ci−1]} ≥
R(x[co+1,ci−1], y[co+1,ci−1])|{p[co+1,ci−1]},
(84)
which has been proved in Theorem 1.
D. Complexity Analysis
As illustrated in Algorithm 1, the proposed DV-MOSS
algorithm is composed of an inner JUBPA routine and an
outer Markov approximation procedure. Given a selected
satellite subset, JUBPA alternates between UA&BA and PA.
In UA/BA, UEs propose the requirements of changing as-
sociated satellites and subcarriers following preference func-
tions until a stable matching is reached. UA&BA has the
complexity of O(ZthJ + JK/Zth). Subsequent PA solves
the power allocation subproblem via dual decomposition. In
PA, each satellite updates its corresponding multipliers and
power variables. This leads to the complexity of O(RPAZth)
where RPA is the number of iterations. Therefore, JUBPA
has the complexity of O(ZthJ + JK/Zth + RPAZth), and
Algorithm 1 will invoke JUBPA C0 times with the complexity
O(C0[ZthJ + JK/Zth + RPAZth]). In each outer iteration, we
have the current satellite subset S1 with its objective value
R1 computed by the inner JUBPA. A candidate subset S2
is generated by exploration, and its objective R2 is obtained
by one additional call to JUBPA. The Markov update then
only computes the performance difference ∆R = R2 −R1,
evaluates the transition probability via (23), draws a uniform
random variable, and selects either S1 or S2 accordingly. The
complexity of these operations is O(1). Thus the per-iteration
cost is dominated by the inner JUBPA evaluation.


--- Page 10 ---
10
(a)
(b)
Fig. 2. Spatial distributions of (a) satellites with UEs and (b) UEs.

[5765.39, 2717.12, 252.39]
V. SIMULATION RESULTS
A. M-LEO System Settings
To evaluate DV-MOSS in a more practical setting, we
consider a Walker Constellation Design Pattern for an M-LEO
satellite communication system. The constellation consists of
1,000 satellites deployed at different orbits with the same alti-
tude of 550 km and a common inclination of 70° but different
right ascension of the ascending nodes. The communication
system operates at a carrier frequency of fc = 6 GHz, and
each subcarrier occupies a bandwidth of 10 MHz. 25 UEs
are uniformly distributed within a circular area of radius
1,500 km, centered at aUEC. The coordinates of the satellites
constellation and UEs are shown in Fig. 2. Throughout the
simulations, unless otherwise specified, we adopt the parame-
ters reported in Table I (see [29] and references therein).
B. DV-MOSS Simulations
1) DV-MOSS Convergence: In Fig.3, we can see that DV-
MOSS, implemented via Algorithm 1, exhibits a consistent
convergence behavior under different shadowing conditions
(SF = 1, 2, and 3 dB). As expected, better channel quality
(smaller shadowing factor) leads to higher achievable sum
rates. Under severe fading scenarios (e.g., SF = 3 dB), it
becomes more challenging to identify satellite groups that
jointly satisfy the system constraints and yield high data rates.
Consequently, the algorithm stabilizes more quickly, leading
to an earlier decay of the exploration probability. Conversely,
under milder fading conditions (e.g., SF = 1 dB), DV-MOSS
continues exploring larger candidate sets, resulting in better
final performance but requiring more iterations to converge.
Fig. 3. Convergence of DV-MOSS with Pmax = 50 W.
Fig. 4. DV-MOSS’s CDF curve with Pmax = 50 W.
Occasional drops may still occur even after the sum rate
reaches high values. This behavior arises from the Markov
approximation. It happens because the algorithm continuously
explores alternative satellite constellations to achieve marginal
performance gains even after achieving a high objective func-
tion value. As a result, satellite groups corresponding to lower
function values can still be found and cause fluctuation. Fig. 4
further illustrates this behavior by showing the cumulative
distribution function (CDF) of the achieved sum rates. The
CDF curves show that smaller shadowing factors yield a
clear rightward shift, indicating consistently higher rates across
UEs. This validates not only the convergence of DV-MOSS but
also its robustness in adapting to different channel conditions.
2) Performance of Matching-game based UA and BA in
DV-MOSS: Fig. 5 illustrates the convergence behavior of the
matching-based UA and BA (Algorithm 3) procedures with
a fixed PA policy. The power of each UE is obtained by
the initialization method of JUBPA. The results show that
the UA-BA combination can converge reliably under different
network sizes (|U| = 20, 25, and 30). Notably, the sum rate
improves significantly after the first iteration, but subsequent
iterations yield limited gains. This is because, without invoking
Algorithm 4, interference cannot be effectively managed. This
limits further performance improvement. Moreover, the sum
rate grows with the number of UEs, since a larger user pool
increases the likelihood of favorable satellite-UE matching and


--- Page 11 ---
11
Fig. 5. Convergence of matching process (rmin = 0.3 Mbps).
Fig. 6. Convergence of JUBPA algorithm.
more efficient bandwidth utilization.
3) Effectiveness of JUBPA in DV-MOSS: Fig. 6 shows the
convergence behavior of the proposed JUBPA algorithm under
different shadowing factors SF = 1, 2, 3 dB. The results show
that JUBPA achieves stable convergence across all channel
conditions. The results are consistent with the trend shown
in Fig. 3 . As expected, better channel quality, corresponding
to smaller SF, leads to higher achievable sum rates, since
weaker shadowing implies less blockage and more reliable
links. Moreover, JUBPA converges rapidly, typically within
two to three iterations. This highlights its efficiency in practical
implementations. Compared with the UA-BA combination,
JUBPA yields a pronounced performance gain, underscoring
the effectiveness of jointly incorporating PA into the matching-
based UA and BA.
C. Performance Comparison
1) Gains of Joint UA-BA Matching in DV-MOSS: In order
to verify the effectiveness of the matching game-based UA
and BA methods, we evaluate the performance of DV-MOSS
against the following two methods: (i) Fixed-UA + Fixed-BA:
both UA and BA are predetermined in a static manner without
applying matching game theory; (ii) Fixed-UA + Matching-
BA: the UA is fixed, and the BA is optimized via the matching
game algorithm (Algorithm 3). These methods are testified
under the same Markov approximation and PA procedures,
ensuring a fair comparison separating the contribution of
Fig. 7.
Comparison among matching algorithms with |U| = 35, φ ∈
h
π
10 , 7π
20
i
.
Fig. 8.
Comparison among selection algorithms with |U| = 35, φ ∈
h
π
10 , 7π
20
i
.
matching game-based UA and BA. The results are presented
in Fig. 7, where the sum rate performance is plotted as a
function of the cone angle φ varying from
π
10 to 7π
20 in a step
size of
π
40. The achievable sum rate generally improves with
larger φ since a wider cone angle allows more satellites to be
considered in the association and beam assignment process.
The performance gaps among three methods highlight the
importance of applying matching game theory to both UA
and BA. Specifically, the double matching scheme consistently
outperforms the baselines, demonstrating the synergistic gain
of jointly adapting both UA and PA. In contrast, Fixed-
UA + Matching-BA method achieves moderate improvement
over the fixed baseline, but its performance saturates when φ
grows as fixing UA limits the ability to exploit the enlarged
candidate satellite set. Finally, the fixed method exhibits the
poorest performance and even suffers performance degradation
at higher φ, where interference becomes more significant and
cannot be effectively mitigated without adaptive UA and BA.
2) Comparative Evaluation of Satellite Selection Strategies
under Dynamic Visibility: To further gain the insight of DV-
MOSS, we conduct comparative experiments that highlight its
capability not only in adaptively selecting satellites, but also in
dynamically determining the number of active satellites with


--- Page 12 ---
12
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
(e)
(f)
(g)
(h)
Fig. 9. Visualized satellite trajectories: (a) 2D at t = 1, (b) 3D at t = 1, (c) 2D at t = 7, (d) 3D at t = 7, (e) 2D at t = 9, (f) 3D at t = 9, (g) 2D at
t = 13, and (h) 3D at t = 13. (φ = π
5 rad).
visibility variations and constellation size constraints. For this
purpose, we select the following four schemes to compare with
our proposed DV-MOSS approach.
• ϵ-Markov [30]: It follows the general framework of the
proposed algorithm. The execution of either the E-stage
or the C-stage is determined in a stochastic manner.
• sφ-nearest: It selects the sφ satellites closest to the UE
cluster center.
• 2-nearest: It always selects the two closest satellites to
the UE cluster center.
• sφ-random: It randomly selects sφ satellites without
considering their locations.
Here, sφ denotes the number of service satellites determined
by DV-MOSS as a function of the cone angle φ. For a fair
comparison, all methods (except the 2-nearest scheme) are
assumed to select the same number of satellites, sφ, with
an identical JUBPA process. The 2-nearest method fixes the
number of service satellites to 2 irrespective of the cone angle.
Fig. 8 presents the sum rates of the proposed DV-MOSS
framework in comparison with the above baselines under
varying cone angles φ ∈
 π
10, 7π
20

for a network with |U| = 35.
From the results, we observe that DV-MOSS consistently
achieves the highest sum rate across the entire range of
cone angles. In particular, the performance gap between DV-
MOSS and the 2-nearest scheme is remarkable. Since the 2-
nearest scheme fixes the number of serving satellites to 2,
it fails to exploit the potential spatial diversity provided by
larger visibility regions when φ increases. By contrast, DV-
MOSS dynamically adapts the number of selected satellites,
thereby striking a balance between interference mitigation and
multi-satellite diversity. This adaptivity translates directly into
substantial throughput gains. Moreover, while the sφ-nearest
and sφ-random baselines partially reflect the constellation
size constraint by fixing the number of selected satellites
to sφ, they ignore the coupled impact of cone-angle-shaped
visibility and interference. As a result, their performance tends
to plateau or even degrade at larger cone angles. By contrast,
DV-MOSS jointly incorporates constellation size and visibility
geometry into the selection and allocation process, maintain-
ing consistently superior performance across all cone-angle
settings. In addition to the above observations, Fig. 8 shows
that ϵ-Markov method achieves competitive but inferior results
compared to DV-MOSS owing to its stochastic decision-
making between exploration and consolidation. This stochastic
behavior prevents consistent exploitation of favorable satellite
constellations. These results collectively demonstrate that the
proposed DV-MOSS framework is not only adaptive in the
number of satellites but also better suited to address visibility
constraints and constellation dynamics than benchmarks.
Finally, to evaluate the influence of the inherent M-LEO
dynamism on the number of selected service satellites, we
simulated the time-varying selection results obtained by DV-
MOSS. Fig. 9 illustrates the constellation snapshot of the
chosen satellites under the proposed workflow, with simulation
results recorded from t = 1 to t = 13 minutes. Two
important observations can be made. First, the number of se-
lected satellites varies continuously over time. This is a direct
consequence of the dynamic evolution of the constellation and
the changes of satellites visibility. Second, as highlighted in
Fig. 9(f) at t = 9 minutes, there exist three visible satellites


--- Page 13 ---
13
that are not selected by DV-MOSS above the service area.
According to the sφ-nearest rule, they should have been
chosen instead of satellites s6,38 and s18,18. This indicates that
merely selecting the geographically closest satellites does not
always guarantee optimal performance, thereby underscoring
the advantage of the proposed strategy.
VI. CONCLUSIONS
We investigated the joint optimization of satellite selection,
UA, BA, and PA in M-LEO constellations under dynamic
visibility constraints. To tackle the inherent NP-hardness of
the formulated problem, we proposed the DV-MOSS frame-
work that integrates Markov approximation with a matching
game-based approach for UA and BA. Simulation results
on the Walker constellation demonstrated that DV-MOSS
achieves stable convergence under various channel conditions
and significantly outperforms four state-of-the-art baselines.
In particular, the proposed algorithm provides, on average,
a 7.85% sum-rate gain over the best-performing benchmark
with affordable computational complexity. Moreover, dynamic
simulations show that the selected service satellites adapt
seamlessly to time-varying constellation visibility, thereby
verifying the robustness and generality of the approach. An
additional insight is that the commonly used closest-satellite
selection rule does not necessarily yield the best performance,
underscoring the necessity of adaptive and visibility-aware
satellite selection in practical M-LEO systems.
